method.vowel_derivation: Vowel rules start from panphon weighted feature-edit distance between the two phonemic inventories (scripts/generate_derived_lens_rules_v1.py) and are then corrected by hand where the metric is known to fail.
method.consonant_derivation: Consonant rules are hand-authored. Feature distance is unreliable for consonants: the perceptually salient mappings are exactly the ones the distance threshold rejects.
method.evidence_policy: No rule in this file claims a cited listener result. Every rule is a derived or hand-authored candidate awaiting both a published source and blind human QC.
method.identity_policy: A category the listener language already owns produces no rule.

profiles.claim: Experimental Italian-through-American-English listener profile. Derived vowel baseline plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC.

vowel_rules.note: Italian /a/ has no exact American English counterpart; /ɑ/ is the nearest category by feature distance (0.25).
Italian close-mid /e/ falls between English /eɪ/ and /ɛ/; the monophthongal /ɛ/ is taken as the nearest non-diphthongal category.
Italian close-mid /o/ maps to the nearest English back rounded category.

consonant_rules.note: American English has no trill; listeners assimilate the Italian trill to the /ɹ/ category. Hand-authored because feature distance rejects the pair (manner difference).
English lacks a phonemic palatal nasal; /n/ is the nearest category. Labeled projection, not a listener result.
English lacks a phonemic palatal lateral; /l/ is the nearest category.
English has no alveolar /dz/ affricate phoneme; the postalveolar /dʒ/ is the nearest category.

prosody_rules.note: Italian yes/no questions carry a final rise; an American-English listener maps the unmarked rise-fall onto a statement-like final fall.

profiles.claim: Experimental English-through-Italian listener profile. Derived vowel baseline (verbatim from generate_derived_lens_rules_v1.py) plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the English voice with Italian lens targets receipted in the en-US map.

vowel_rules.note: Italian has no lax /ɪ/; nearest category is /i/ (feature distance 0.25).
Italian lacks /æ/; nearest category is /e/ (feature distance 0.5).
Nearest Italian category to English /ɑ/ is /a/ (feature distance 0.25).
Italian has no lax /ʊ/; the metric's nearest category is /ɔ/ (feature distance 0.5). Kept verbatim from the deriver; blind QC may prefer /u/.
Italian lacks /ʌ/; nearest category by the metric is /e/ (feature distance 0.5).
Italian has no /ɜ/ nurse vowel; nearest category is /ɛ/ (feature distance 0.5).
Italian has no reduced schwa; nearest full-vowel category is /ɛ/ (feature distance 0.5).

consonant_rules.note: Italian lacks /θ/; listeners assimilate it to /t/. Hand-authored: feature distance wrongly prefers /s/.
Italian lacks /ð/; listeners assimilate it to /d/. Hand-authored: feature distance wrongly prefers /z/.
Italian has no phonemic /ŋ/; the nearest category is /n/. Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /ɲ/.
Italian has no approximant rhotic; listeners assimilate English /ɹ/ to the trill /r/. Hand-authored because feature distance rejects the pair (manner).
Italian lacks phonemic /ʒ/; the nearest category is /ʃ/ (feature distance also agrees at 0.25).

insertion_rules.note: Italian content words end in vowels; listeners resolve a word-final consonant with a paragogic vowel (cat -> 'catte'). Widened past the obstruent-only trigger because the constraint is on any final consonant.

prosody_rules.note: Italian stress is overwhelmingly penultimate; an English initial-secondary/late-primary word is pulled toward the Italian pattern.

profiles.claim: Experimental English-through-German listener profile. Derived monophthong-vowel baseline plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the English voice with German lens targets receipted in the en-US map. English diphthongs are intentionally not recategorized: German already parses /aɪ aʊ ɔʏ/ natively, and the deriver's cross-diphthong pairings (for example oʊ->ɔʏ) are metric artifacts rather than listener judgements.

vowel_rules.note: German lacks /æ/; nearest category is /e/ (feature distance 0.5).
Nearest German category to English /ɑ/ is /a/ (feature distance 0.25).
Nearest German category to English /ʌ/ is /ə/ (feature distance 0.25).
German has no /ɜ/ nurse vowel; nearest category is /ə/ (feature distance 0.0).

consonant_rules.note: German has no /w/; listeners assimilate it to /v/ (the signature 've' for 'we'). Hand-authored: feature distance wrongly prefers /j/.
German lacks /θ/; listeners assimilate it to /s/ ('sink' for 'think'). Feature distance agrees at 0.5.
German lacks /ð/; listeners assimilate it to /z/ ('zis' for 'this'). Feature distance agrees at 0.5.
German has no approximant rhotic; listeners assimilate English /ɹ/ to the uvular /ʁ/. Hand-authored because feature distance rejects the pair (place).

insertion_rules.note: German gives vowel-initial words a glottal onset; listeners impose it on English (an apple -> [ʔæn ʔæpəl]). Structural, applies at the word edge only.

prosody_rules.note: German lexical stress is strongly root-initial; a late English primary competing with an initial secondary is pulled forward.

profiles.claim: Experimental German-through-American-English listener profile. Derived vowel baseline (panphon feature distance between the two phonemic inventories) plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the German Katja voice with English lens targets receipted in the de-DE map. espeak surfaces the German rhotic as trill/tap rather than the phonemic uvular, so the rhotic rules are authored against r and ɾ. The deriver's ts→tʃ is excluded: English owns /ts/ as a sequence and the map expands the espeak ʦ ligature identically on both sides, so that rule would render no difference.

vowel_rules.note: Nearest en-US category to de-DE /e/ by panphon weighted feature edit distance (0.25). Like the Italian close-mid /e/, the monophthongal /ɛ/ is taken as the nearest non-diphthongal category; blind QC may prefer /eɪ/.
German short /a/ has no exact American counterpart; /ɑ/ is the nearest category by feature distance (0.25).
Nearest en-US category to de-DE /o/ by feature distance (0.25); the monophthongal /ɔ/ is taken over /oʊ/, matching the Italian /o/ decision. Blind QC may prefer /oʊ/.
English has no front rounded /y/; nearest category is /u/ (feature distance 0.5).
English has no lax front rounded /ʏ/; nearest category is /ɪ/ (feature distance 0.5).
English has no front rounded /ø/; nearest category is /ɛ/ (feature distance 0.75).
English has no lax front rounded /œ/; nearest category is /ɛ/ (feature distance 0.5).
The deriver pairs phonemic /ɔʏ/->/ɔɪ/ at 0.5; the rule is authored against the espeak surface form ɔø (Leute, neu). English owns the /ɔɪ/ diphthong natively ('boy').

consonant_rules.note: English has no /ç/; listeners assimilate the ich-laut to /ʃ/ ('ich' -> 'ish'). Hand-authored: feature distance rejects the pair (1.375, over threshold). Source is the adapter's NFD surface c + combining cedilla (U+0327); the de-DE map's locale-gated NFC compose step renders the neutral side as precomposed ç.
English has no /x/; listeners hear /k/ ('Bach' -> 'back'). Feature distance agrees at exactly the 1.0 threshold.

English has no trill; listeners assimilate espeak's German trill to /ɹ/. Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /ʒ/ for the phonemic uvular (1.125, over threshold), which is noise, not a listener judgement.
espeak's intervocalic German rhotic is the tap /ɾ/; English listeners hear the approximant /ɹ/. Hand-authored: the same category judgement as the trill.
English has no /pf/ cluster-initial affricate; listeners drop the stop and hear /f/ ('Pferd' -> 'ferd'). Hand-authored: feature distance is meaningless on the cluster (2.75 to /tʃ/).

prosody_rules.note: German yes/no questions carry a final rise; an American-English listener maps it onto a statement-like final fall.

profiles.claim: Experimental Castilian-Spanish-through-American-English listener profile. Derived vowel baseline (panphon feature distance between the two phonemic inventories) plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the Spanish Elvira voice with English lens targets receipted in the es-ES map. Two Spanish surface segments carry no rule by design: ð (English already owns /ð/) and the tap ɾ (English owns the flap allophonically in AmE 'city'), so a rule there would render no difference. Authored against the espeak surface inventory, which realizes /b d ɡ/ as [β ð ɣ] except after nasals.

vowel_rules.note: Spanish /a/ has no exact American counterpart; /ɑ/ is the nearest category by feature distance (0.25).
Spanish close-mid /e/ falls between English /eɪ/ and /ɛ/; the monophthongal /ɛ/ is taken as the nearest non-diphthongal category, matching the Italian /e/ decision.
Spanish close-mid /o/ maps to the nearest English back rounded category, matching the Italian /o/ decision. Blind QC may prefer /oʊ/.

consonant_rules.note: English listeners hear the jota as /h/ ('jalapeño' -> 'hala-penyo'). Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /k/ at exactly the 1.0 threshold; matches the pt-to-en dorsal-r mapping to /h/.
English lacks a phonemic palatal nasal; listeners parse ñ as /n(j)/ ('niño' -> 'neen-yo'). Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /ŋ/ (0.5). Matches the it-to-en ɲ rule.

English lacks a phonemic palatal lateral; the conservative Castilian /ʎ/ files under /l/, matching the it-to-en ʎ rule (the metric agreed at 1.5 but over threshold). The common AmE 'tortiya' percept reflects the yeísta /ʝ/, not this lateral.

American English has no trill; listeners assimilate the Spanish trill to the /ɹ/ category. Hand-authored because feature distance rejects the pair (nearest /l/ at 1.25 is noise). Matches the it-to-en trill rule.

English has no bilabial fricative; listeners file espeak's intervocalic [β] under /v/ ('La Habana' -> 'Havana'). Hand-authored: the phonemic deriver never sees [β] (Spanish /b/ exists in both inventories).
English has no voiced velar fricative; listeners file espeak's intervocalic [ɣ] under /ɡ/ ('amigo'). Hand-authored: same phonemic-invisibility as [β].

prosody_rules.note: Spanish yes/no questions are marked by intonation alone; an American-English listener maps the rise onto a statement-like final fall.

profiles.claim: Experimental English-through-Castilian-Spanish listener profile. Derived vowel baseline plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the English voice with Spanish lens targets receipted in the en-US map. Castilian inventory: English /θ/ is a category Spanish already owns (distinción), so no rule fires on it — Latin American Spanish (seseo, yeísmo) would need its own profile. Of the English diphthongs only /oʊ/ is recategorized (the attested monophthongization to /o/, 'boat' -> 'bot'); /eɪ aɪ aʊ ɔɪ/ parse natively for a Spanish listener (espeak itself emits A/I/W for reina/hay/pausa) and are excluded on the same grounds as the German diphthongs.

vowel_rules.note: Spanish has no lax /ɪ/; nearest category is /i/ (feature distance 0.25). The signature 'I speek Eenglish'.
Spanish has no open /ɛ/; nearest category is /e/ (feature distance 0.25).
Spanish lacks /æ/; nearest category is /a/ (feature distance 0.5).
Nearest Spanish category to English /ɑ/ is /a/ (feature distance 0.25).
Nearest Spanish category to English /ɔ/ is /o/ (feature distance 0.25).
Spanish has no lax /ʊ/; the metric's nearest category is /o/ (feature distance 0.75). Kept verbatim from the deriver; blind QC may prefer /u/.
Spanish lacks /ʌ/; nearest category is /a/ (feature distance 0.5).
Spanish has no /ɜ/ nurse vowel; nearest category is /a/ (feature distance 0.75).
Spanish has no reduced schwa; nearest full-vowel category is /a/ (feature distance 0.75).

Spanish has no /ou/ diphthong; listeners hear /o/ ('boat' -> 'bot'). Hand-authored against the Misaki GOAT shorthand: feature distance is meaningless across the diphthong boundary (7.25). The other English diphthongs parse natively in Spanish and carry no rule.

consonant_rules.note: Spanish merges orthographic v with /b/; feature distance wrongly prefers /f/. Deriver override retained; needs a citation before claiming a direct tier.
Castilian Spanish has no voiced /z/; listeners devoice it to /s/ ('zoo' -> 'su'). Feature distance agrees at 0.25.
Spanish has no /dʒ/; listeners hear /tʃ/ ('jeep' -> 'chip'). Feature distance agrees at 0.5.

Spanish owns [ð] only as an allophone of /d/ (dedo), so the listener's phoneme is /d/ ('this' -> 'dis'). Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /θ/, which would map a voiced fricative onto the voiceless one.
Spanish has no /ʃ/; listeners hear /s/ ('ship' -> 'sip'; the ship~chip confusion is also attested). Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /θ/ (0.75).
Spanish has no /ʒ/; listeners hear /s/ ('vision' -> 'bision'). Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /θ/ at exactly the 1.0 threshold.
Castilian Spanish has no phonemic /ŋ/; the nearest category is /n/. Hand-authored: feature distance prefers /ɲ/ (0.5). Matches the it-to-en ŋ decision.
Spanish has no /h/; listeners hear the jota /x/ ('hat' -> 'jat'). Hand-authored: the metric's nearest at 2.375 (/l/) is noise.

Spanish has no approximant rhotic; listeners assimilate English /ɹ/ to the trill /r/. Hand-authored because feature distance rejects the pair (nearest /j/ at 1.625). Matches the it-to-en and de-to-en rhotic decisions.

insertion_rules.note: Spanish forbids word-initial /s/+consonant; listeners prepend /e/ (school -> 'eschool', Spain -> 'España'). Structural phonotactic repair, not a segment substitution.

prosody_rules.note: Spanish stress is overwhelmingly penultimate (llana); an English initial-secondary/late-primary word is pulled toward it.

profiles.claim: Experimental English-through-French listener profile. Derived vowel baseline corrected by hand plus hand-authored consonant recategorizations; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the English voice with French lens targets already receipted in the en-US map. Three rules are kept although the renderer collapses them (ɪ->i, ɑ->a, ɹ->ʁ): they are the correct recategorizations and are reported as renderer-inaudible rather than deleted, so the absence of the signature French R is visible instead of silent.

vowel_rules.note: French lacks /æ/; listeners use /a/ (cat -> [kat]). Hand-corrected: feature distance preferred /e/, which would render 'cat' as 'ket'.
French has no lax /ʊ/; listeners use cardinal /u/ (book -> [buk]). Hand-corrected: feature distance preferred /ɔ/, a height error.
Nearest French category to English /ʌ/ is /ə/.
French has no /ɜ/ nurse vowel; nearest category is /ə/.
French has no lax /ɪ/; listeners use /i/ (ship/sheep merge). Correct rule, but the English voice renders /ɪ/ and /i/ identically, so it is reported as renderer-inaudible.
Nearest French category to English /ɑ/ is /a/. The English voice collapses the pair, so it is reported as renderer-inaudible.

consonant_rules.note: French lacks /θ/; listeners assimilate it to /s/ (think -> 'sink').
French lacks /ð/; listeners assimilate it to /z/ (this -> 'zis').
French /ŋ/ occurs only in loanwords; listeners resolve it to /n/.
French has no native /ʧ/; the affricate is simplified to its fricative release (chair -> 'share').
French has no native /ʤ/; the affricate is simplified to /ʒ/.

French listeners recategorize the English approximant as their uvular /ʁ/. This is the signature effect of the direction and the English voice renders both identically, so it is reported as renderer-inaudible rather than silently applied.
French /h/ is silent (haricot -> [aʁiko]); listeners do not perceive the segment.

prosody_rules.note: French has no lexical stress; prominence falls on the final syllable of the rhythmic group, pulling an English word's primary rightward.

profiles.claim: Experimental French-through-English listener profile. Derived vowel baseline corrected by hand; no cited listener result and no blind QC. Renders on the French voice with English lens targets receipted in the fr-FR map. The uvular-R rule is kept although the French voice collapses it, and reported as renderer-inaudible: it is the correct recategorization and the direction should not appear to be doing something it cannot.

vowel_rules.note: English has no front rounded /y/; listeners hear the nearest back rounded vowel (tu -> 'too'). The signature effect of the direction.
English lacks /ø/; unrounding it yields /e/. Hand-corrected: the deriver sent both /ø/ and /œ/ to /ɛ/, merging two French categories the listener does distinguish.
English lacks /œ/; unrounding it yields /ɛ/.
French /a/ is fronter than any English low vowel; listeners assimilate to /ɑ/.
English has no pure monophthong /e/; listeners use /ɛ/.
English has no pure monophthong /o/; listeners use /ɔ/.

consonant_rules.note: English listeners recategorize the French uvular /ʁ/ as their alveolar approximant. The French voice renders both identically, so it is reported as renderer-inaudible.
English resolves an illegal /ps/ or /pf/ onset by dropping the stop (psychologie).
English resolves an illegal /ts/ onset by dropping the stop (tsar -> [zɑɹ]).

prosody_rules.note: French prominence is phrase-final and non-lexical; an English listener imposes a word-level primary, pulling it off the final syllable.
